--- SOURCE 3: Biodiversity in agriculture - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biodiversity_in_agriculture

SUMMARY:
Biodiversity in agriculture is the measure of biodiversity found on agricultural land. Biodiversity is the total diversity of species present in an area.

FULL CONTENT:
![](/static/images/icons/wikipedia.png)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)
![The Free Encyclopedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-tagline-en.svg)

## Contents

# Biodiversity in agriculture

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/AgricultureInHaholaValley.jpg/250px-AgricultureInHaholaValley.jpg)

**Biodiversity in agriculture** is the measure of biodiversity found on agricultural land. [Biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity") is the total diversity of species present in an area at all [levels of biological organization](/wiki/Levels_of_biological_organization "Levels of biological organization").[[1]](#cite_note-Harlan_2012-1) It is characterized by heterogeneous habitats that support the diverse ecological structure.[[1]](#cite_note-Harlan_2012-1) In agricultural areas, biodiversity decreases as varying landscapes are lost and native plants are replaced with cultivated [crops](/wiki/Crop "Crop").[[1]](#cite_note-Harlan_2012-1) Increasing [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity") in agriculture can increase the [sustainability](/wiki/Sustainability "Sustainability") of farms through the restoration of [ecosystem services](/wiki/Ecosystem_service "Ecosystem service") that aid in regulating agricultural lands.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Biodiversity in agriculture can be increased through the process of agro[ecological restoration](/wiki/Ecological_restoration "Ecological restoration"), as farm biodiversity is an aspect of [agroecology](/wiki/Agroecology "Agroecology").

Biodiversity is the measure of biotic and abiotic diversity in an ecosystem, described by heterogeneity.[[1]](#cite_note-Harlan_2012-1) The loss of biodiversity in agriculture has been an increasing issue since the global increase of food demands and success of popular crops.[[3]](#cite_note-Benton_2003-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Khoury_2014-4) This loss of heterogeneity declines species biodiversity on agricultural lands.[[5]](#cite_note-Martin_2020-5) Biodiversity in agriculture is essential in providing ecosystem services, which conserves biodiversity while providing agricultural services.[[6]](#cite_note-Robertson_2014-6)

## Biodiversity loss

Agriculture creates a conflict over the [use of land](/wiki/Land_use "Land use") between wildlife and humans.[[7]](#cite_note-Feber_2007-7) Land use for agriculture has been a driving force in creating [biodiversity loss](/wiki/Biodiversity_loss "Biodiversity loss")[[8]](#cite_note-8) An increase in the amount of [pasture](/wiki/Pasture "Pasture") and [crop land](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land") over the last few hundred years has led to the rapid loss of natural [habitats](/wiki/Habitat "Habitat").[[7]](#cite_note-Feber_2007-7) The [Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations](/wiki/Food_and_Agriculture_Organization_of_the_United_Nations "Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations") estimates that more than 40% of Earth's land surface is currently used for agriculture. Because so much land has been converted to agriculture, [habitat loss](/wiki/Habitat_loss "Habitat loss") is recognized as the driving force in biodiversity loss.[[9]](#cite_note-Jackson_2002-9) A decline in farmland biodiversity can be traced to changes in farming practices and increased agricultural intensity.[[3]](#cite_note-Benton_2003-3): 182 Nonetheless, according to the FAO, "biodiversity is just as important on farms and in fields as it is in deep river valleys or mountain cloud forests".[[10]](#cite_note-FAO-10) In recent years, the world has acknowledged the value of biodiversity through treaties formed, such as in the 1992 [Convention on Biological Diversity](/wiki/Convention_on_Biological_Diversity "Convention on Biological Diversity").[[10]](#cite_note-FAO-10)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Tractors_in_Potato_Field.jpg/250px-Tractors_in_Potato_Field.jpg)

The loss of habitat connectivity caused by [fragmentation](/wiki/Habitat_fragmentation "Habitat fragmentation") in agricultural areas threatens biodiversity, as it decreases population sizes and restricts its access to external resources.[[11]](#cite_note-Banaszek_2011-11) Species facing [habitat fragmentation](/wiki/Habitat_fragmentation "Habitat fragmentation") can also create a [genetic bottleneck](/wiki/Genetic_bottleneck "Genetic bottleneck")[[12]](#cite_note-Geffen_2007-12) The decreased gene pool threatens species through factors such as [inbreeding depression](/wiki/Inbreeding_depression "Inbreeding depression"), where the less advantageous populations lowers the species survival rates.[[11]](#cite_note-Banaszek_2011-11) [Monoculture](/wiki/Monoculture "Monoculture") is the practice of producing a single crop on a given piece of land, including [crop rotation](/wiki/Crop_rotation "Crop rotation").[[12]](#cite_note-Geffen_2007-12) While monoculture produces optimum yields, it has implications for the biodiversity of farms.[[9]](#cite_note-Jackson_2002-9) [Heterogeneity](/wiki/Heterogeneity "Heterogeneity"), the diversity of the landscape, has been shown to be associated with species diversity. For example, butterfly abundance has been found to increase with heterogeneity. Land that is not cropped, such as fallow land, grass margins in the spaces between different fields, and strips of scrub along field boundaries increase heterogeneity and thus the biodiversity of a farm. Plants attract insects, which will attract certain species of birds, and those birds will attract their natural predators. The cover provided by non-cropped land allows species to move across the landscape.[[3]](#cite_note-Benton_2003-3): 183–184 In Asian rice, one study showed crop diversification by growing flowering crops in strips beside rice fields could reduce pests so that insecticide spraying was reduced by 70%, yields increase by 5%, together resulting in an economic advantage of 7.5%.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## The Green Revolution

One of the issues facing biodiversity in areas of industrial agriculture is the loss of [heterogeneity](/wiki/Heterogeneity "Heterogeneity"), described by the loss of a biotic and abiotic diversity.[[1]](#cite_note-Harlan_2012-1)[[3]](#cite_note-Benton_2003-3) Since 1966, the [Green Revolution](/wiki/Green_Revolution "Green Revolution") enhanced agricultural productivity through technological, economical, and political advancements in an effort to increase food security globally.[[14]](#cite_note-Pingali_2012-14) This includes the introduction of [genetically modified crops](/wiki/Genetically_modified_crop "Genetically modified crop"), which allows for increased yield, pest resistance, and improved crop varieties.[[14]](#cite_note-Pingali_2012-14) These advancements also led to increased global geographical spread of 52 agricultural crops with cereals such as wheat, rice, and maize showing the greatest increase in the past 50 years.[[4]](#cite_note-Khoury_2014-4) The loss of agricultural heterogeneity decreases local food security due to a loss in crop diversity, despite its accommodation of global food demands.[[4]](#cite_note-Khoury_2014-4)

## Heterogeneity

Heterogeneity is essential in increasing species heterogeneity, which maintain stable ecological structures essential to providing ecosystem services.[[5]](#cite_note-Martin_2020-5) Of the features associated with species diversity is land size, where a study proved a relationship between smaller agricultural fields and increased species richness.[[15]](#cite_note-Boulinier_1998-15) The area of an agricultural field is associated with organisms accessibility to the edges of the field, which usually allow access for fields with different biophysical and geophysical features.[[15]](#cite_note-Boulinier_1998-15) Increased accessibility to a diverse ecological features increases heterogeneity and reduces edge effects on populations inhabiting agricultural fields.[[3]](#cite_note-Benton_2003-3)[[15]](#cite_note-Boulinier_1998-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Ecosystem services

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Pollination_-_Apis_mellifera_on_Taraxacum_officinale.jpg/250px-Pollination_-_Apis_mellifera_on_Taraxacum_officinale.jpg)

Agriculture is a transformative process to any habitat, with a main focus on cultivating crops for human consumption.[[17]](#cite_note-Swinton_2007-17) Views on ecosystem services can be presented through viewpoints that benefit humans environmentally, economically, and culturally to motivate the practices that support ecosystem services in the agricultural industry.[[17]](#cite_note-Swinton_2007-17) For example, low crop diversity can increase pests and their resistance to pesticides, resulting in large ecological disturbances and economical losses.[[6]](#cite_note-Robertson_2014-6) This can be mitigated with increased crop rotation, which contributes to more diverse soil microbiota and insects that provide ecosystem services.[[6]](#cite_note-Robertson_2014-6) These mass flowering crops also provide pulses of resources to generalist pollinators, including honey bees.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Another example is the conservation of pollinators such as honeybees that can contribute to the agricultural industry, where contributing to the increase of pollinators is reciprocated with increased crop production.[[17]](#cite_note-Swinton_2007-17)

--- SOURCE 5: Biodiversity - Agriculture and rural development - European Union ---
URL: https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/cap-my-country/sustainability/environmental-sustainability/biodiversity_en

SUMMARY:
The common agricultural policy sets out to safeguard biodiversity within sustainable systems of farming; ensuring that farmers can produce food and earn a living whilst protecting agricultural ecosystems. The common agricultural policy (CAP) has among its objectives to contribute to halting and reversing biodiversity loss, enhancing ecosystem services and preserving habitats and landscapes. Enhancing agricultural biodiversity is also essential when it comes to achieving a sustainable food system for the EU, as set out in the farm to fork strategy. Under CAP rules, eco-schemes and rural development commitments support farmers who adopt or maintain farming practices that contribute to EU environmental and climate goals. The European (EU) CAP Network facilitates the sharing of ideas and good practices for agricultural ecosystems through thematic work on eco-schemes, the green architecture, and pollinators.

6.   Enhancing biodiversity

Enhancing agricultural biodiversity
===================================

The common agricultural policy aims to enhance the variety of species, habitats, pollinators, and landscape features found in the farmland ecosystems of the EU.

![Image 3](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/sites/default/files/styles/oe_theme_medium_no_crop/public/2021-05/banner-enhancing-biodiversity_555x325px_0.jpg?itok=glAY7k7k)

Agricultural biodiversity encompasses all wild and domesticated forms of life found on farms, from plant varieties and breeds of animals, to soil organisms, pests and pollinators.

The common agricultural policy sets out to safeguard biodiversity within sustainable systems of farming; ensuring that farmers can produce food and earn a living whilst protecting agricultural ecosystems.

Page contents

Page contents

*   [Agricultural biodiversity in the EU](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/cap-my-country/sustainability/environmental-sustainability/biodiversity_en#agricultural-biodiversity-in-the-eu)
*   [EU biodiversity strategy](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/cap-my-country/sustainability/environmental-sustainability/biodiversity_en#eu-biodiversity-strategy)

Agricultural biodiversity in the EU
-----------------------------------

Agriculture and biodiversity are strongly interrelated:

*   **Biodiversity relies on agriculture**: In the EU, agriculture supports and shapes a wide variety of habitats, plants, pollinators, animals, fungi and microorganisms. According to the European environment agency (EEA), 50% of all species in the EU rely upon on agricultural habitats. The EEA identified 63 habitat types that depend upon, or can proﬁt from, agricultural activities – mainly low-intensity grazing and mowing.
*   **Agriculture relies on biodiversity**: The production of food and fibre depend upon a variety of ecosystem services, such as soil and water conservation, maintenance of soil fertility, resistance to pests and diseases, pollination of crops and diversity of genetic resources. A number of these services are also essential for mitigating and adapting to climate change and environmental pressures.

![Image 4](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/sites/default/files/styles/oe_theme_ratio_3_2_medium/public/2024-05/compass_600x400px_0.jpg?h=6377f7ce&itok=EtG98BA0)

*   News article
*   17 May 2024

[Commission launches new online tool on sustainability in agriculture](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/media/news/commission-launches-new-online-tool-sustainability-agriculture-2024-05-17_en)

The European Commission has launched the Agri Sustainability Compass, an interactive tool providing comprehensive information on sustainability in agriculture and rural areas.

### Halting and reversing biodiversity loss

However, agricultural biodiversity is in decline across the EU. While there are several contributing factors – including land use change, pollution, climate change, and the impact of invasive species - the decline is also related to some intensive agricultural practices:

*   the abandonment of agricultural activity is a major risk for biodiversity decline, leading to the disintegration of farmland features, landscape homogenisation and soil degradation; this results for example to the disappearance of certain biodiversity-rich habitats such as permanent grasslands;
*   practices associated with intensive farming systems can be harmful to biodiversity, including for example, monoculture, under-rotation, overuse of fertilisers and pesticides, heavy mechanisation;
*   the removal of landscape features and loss of habitats such as hedges, stone walls, terraces, rough grass margins, woodlots, trees, ponds, old buildings.

The common agricultural policy (CAP) has among its objectives to contribute to halting and reversing biodiversity loss, enhancing ecosystem services and preserving habitats and landscapes.

Through the CAP, the European Commission aims to support farmers to:

*   restore, conserve and enhance biodiversity and pollinator populations in their farms;
*   preserve and maintain landscape features;
*   conserve and valorise diverse genetic resources;
*   facilitate the wide array of ecosystem services made possible by biodiversity.

> Agricultural biodiversity is a broad term that includes all components of biological diversity of relevance to food and agriculture, and all components of biological diversity that constitute the agro-ecosystem: the variety and variability of animals, plants and micro-organisms, at the genetic, species and ecosystem levels, which are necessary to sustain key functions of the agro-ecosystem, its structure and processes.
> 
> Conference of the Parties (COP) to the Convention on Biological Diversity, COP Decision V/5

EU biodiversity strategy
------------------------

The European Commission aims to ensure that agriculture can make a strong contribution to the EU’s [biodiversity strategy](https://environment.ec.europa.eu/strategy/biodiversity-strategy-2030_en), one of the central components of the [European Green Deal](https://commission.europa.eu/strategy-and-policy/priorities-2019-2024/european-green-deal_en). The biodiversity strategy sets out a number of key targets related to agriculture:

*   expanding the [Natura 2000 network](https://environment.ec.europa.eu/topics/nature-and-biodiversity/natura-2000_en) so that 30% of EU land is protected;
*   placing at least 10% of agricultural area in the EU under high-diversity landscape features;
*   placing at least 25% of agricultural land under [organic farming](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/farming/organic-farming/organics-glance_en);
*   reducing nutrient loss from fertilisers by at least 50% and reducing the risk and use of chemical pesticides by 50%;
*   reverse the decline of wild pollinators by 2030.

Enhancing agricultural biodiversity is also essential when it comes to achieving a sustainable food system for the EU, as set out in the [farm to fork strategy](https://food.ec.europa.eu/horizontal-topics/farm-fork-strategy_en).

CAP actions
-----------

The CAP promotes sustainable agricultural systems in the EU, enabling farmers to:

*   ![Image 5](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/sites/default/files/styles/oe_theme_medium_no_crop/public/2024-08/icon-sustainability-hand-plant.png?itok=Q6YOrWPo)provide safe, healthy, and sustainably-produced food for society;   
*   ![Image 6](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/sites/default/files/styles/oe_theme_medium_no_crop/public/2024-08/icon-sustainability-hand-money.png?itok=HZVPUH7b)earn a stable and fair income, taking into account the full range of public goods they provide;   
*   ![Image 7](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/sites/default/files/styles/oe_theme_medium_no_crop/public/2024-08/icon-sustainability-hand-globe.png?itok=kWFCmdje)protect natural resources, enhance biodiversity, and contribute to the fight against climate change.   

The rules and measures of the CAP help farmers to protect local agroecosystems and encourage the uptake of biodiversity-friendly practices.

The CAP 2023-27 entered into force on 1 January 2023. It is designed to boost agriculture’s contribution to the goals of the EU’s biodiversity strategy.

### CAP specific objective

One of the [ten specific objectives](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/common-agricultural-policy/cap-overview/cap-glance/key-policy-objectives-cap-2023-27_en) of the CAP 2023-27 focuses on restoring biodiversity through enhancing landscape features, ecosystem services, and genetic resources.

### CAP Strategic Plans

With [CAP Strategic Plans](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/cap-my-country/cap-strategic-plans_en), EU countries have more flexibility to meet the needs of local ecosystems while contributing to shared EU goals for biodiversity.

![Image 8](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/sites/default/files/styles/oe_theme_medium_no_crop/public/2022-06/cap-strategic-plans.jpg?itok=YKXg-7DV)

EU countries’ planned actions

EU countries’ CAP Strategic Plans combine a wide range of targeted interventions to address their specific needs and deliver tangible results.

The plans must contribute to, and be consistent with, EU legislation and commitments relating to climate and environment.

[CAP Strategic Plans by country](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/cap-my-country/cap-strategic-plans_en#cap-strategic-plans-by-country)

### Conditionality

Under [conditionality](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/common-agricultural-policy/income-support/conditionality_en) rules, beneficiaries of the CAP must meet a set of statutory management requirements (SMRs) and good agricultural and environmental conditions (GAECs). A number of these rules focus on protecting farmland ecosystems:

*   farmers must comply with EU directives on the conservation of wild birds (SMR 3) and natural habitats (SMR 4), which involves protecting Natura 2000 areas;
*   requirements to comply with EU directives on nitrates (SMR 2) and pesticides (SMRs 7 and 8) that also protect biodiversity;
*   under GAEC 8, farmers must ensure the retention of landscape features on agricultural land as chosen by EU countries such as walls, hedges, banks, watercourses and trees;
*   other GAECs safeguard [soil](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/cap-my-country/sustainability/environmental-sustainability/natural-resources/soil_en) and [water](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/cap-my-country/sustainability/environmental-sustainability/natural-resources/water_en), bringing knock-on benefits for biodiversity.

### Eco-schemes and rural development

Under CAP rules, [eco-schemes](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/common-agricultural-policy/income-support/eco-schemes_en) and [rural development](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/index_en) commitments support farmers who adopt or maintain farming practices that contribute to EU environmental and climate goals. Through eco-schemes and rural development commitments, the EU rewards farmers for preserving natural resources and providing public goods, which are benefits to the public that are not reflected in market prices and can include practices that support biodiversity.

Interventions supported under the CAP that help enhance biodiversity, are:

*   area-based commitments to multi-annual and/or annual practices such as maintaining high nature value grassland, adopting extensive grazing, breeding traditional plant varieties, and preserving wetlands as important biodiversity habitats;
*   Natura 2000 payments to compensate farmers and foresters for additional costs and income foregone when implementing the [Birds and Habitats Directives](https://environment.ec.europa.eu/topics/nature-and-biodiversity/birds-directive_en);
*   investment measures used towards establishing landscape features, drawing up nature management plans, and purchasing biodiversity-friendly machinery;
*   support to organic farming and forestry to improve the state of ecosystems.

The [European (EU) CAP Network](https://eu-cap-network.ec.europa.eu/index_en) facilitates the sharing of ideas and good practices for agricultural ecosystems through [thematic work](https://eu-cap-network.ec.europa.eu/topics/sustainability_en) on [eco-schemes](https://eu-cap-network.ec.europa.eu/search_en?fulltext=eco-schemes), the [green architecture](https://eu-cap-network.ec.europa.eu/search_en?fulltext=green+architecture), and [pollinators](https://eu-cap-network.ec.europa.eu/search_en?fulltext=pollinators).

*   ![Image 9: biodiversity_innovation-action_320x213px.jpg](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/sites/default/files/styles/oe_theme_medium_no_crop/public/2021-06/biodiversity_innovation-action_320x213px_0.jpg?itok=6qLREnYD)The [agricultural European innovation partnership](https://eu-cap-network.ec.europa.eu/projects_en) (EIP-AGRI) supports projects, focus groups, and operational groups that transform innovative ideas into practical solutions. For example, the EIP-AGRI [focus group on high nature value farming](https://eu-cap-network.ec.europa.eu/publications/eip-agri-focus-group-high-nature-value-farming-profitability-final-report_en) examined how improved cooperation, technology, and management systems could improve the social and economic sustainability of HNV farming without losing its environmental value.   

### Green architecture

The green architecture for the CAP 2023-27 incorporates stronger rules and offers more opportunities for biodiversity-friendly farming.

*   For example, an extra portion of the CAP’s budget is set aside for eco-schemes, which can support voluntary practices undertaken by farmers.
*   At the same time, EU countries are obliged to reserve at least 35% of their rural development budget for environmental and climate-related practices.

![Image 10: CSP overview facts and figures](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/sites/default/files/styles/oe_theme_small_no_crop/public/2023-04/csp-overview-thumbnail_en_0.png?itok=p0_J9Z8j)

*   General publications
*   26 June 2023

Approved 28 CAP Strategic Plans (2023-27)

This document provides a detailed overview of the 27 EU members’ key elements and choices in their approved national plans. It also responds to requests for information to summarise what plans contain and aim to deliver.

English

(5.49 MB - PDF)

[Preview](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/document/download/7b3a0485-c335-4e1b-a53a-9fe3733ca48f_en?filename=approved-28-cap-strategic-plans-2023-27.pdf)[Download](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/document/download/7b3a0485-c335-4e1b-a53a-9fe3733ca48f_en?filename=approved-28-cap-strategic-plans-2023-27.pdf)

Monitoring progress
-------------------

As part of the [common monitoring and evaluation framework](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/common-agricultural-policy/cap-overview/cmef_en) (CMEF), the Commission collects a wide range of indicators and undertakes frequent policy evaluations and studies, which can help to determine the success of the CAP in reaching its objectives for biodiversity:

*   the Commission’s agri-food data portal includes a dashboard with some of the most relevant [indicators for biodiversity](https://agridata.ec.europa.eu/extensions/DashboardIndicators/Biodiversity.html);
*   in November 2019, an independent evaluation of the [impact of the CAP on habitats, landscapes, and biodiversity](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/common-agricultural-policy/cap-overview/cmef/sustainability/impact-cap-habitats-landscapes-biodiversity_en) was published on behalf of the Commission.

Under the Commission’s proposals, the CAP 2023-27 is to include a reinforced performance monitoring and evaluation framework, which will facilitate greater accountability and the transition to a performance-based delivery model.

Knowledge, research, and innovation
-----------------------------------

The Commission supports [research and innovation on ecological approaches and organic farming](https://research-and-innovation.ec.europa.eu/research-area/agriculture-forestry-and-rural-areas/ecological-approaches-and-organic-farming_en) to unlock modern systems of farming that can be productive, profitable, and biodiversity-friendly.

The [farm advisory system](https://agriculture.ec.europa.eu/farming/fas_en) advises farmers how to comply with rules and requirements to protect biodiversity on their farms, while also spreading knowledge of new methods and technology that can benefit biodiversity.

Legal bases
-----------

Conditionality is governed by:

*   rules on support for Strategic Plans drawn up by EU countries under the common agricultural policy ([Regulation (EU) 2021/2115](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2021/2115/oj)) ([Delegated Regulation (EU) 2022/126](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg_del/2022/126/oj));
*   rules on the financing, management and monitoring of the common agricultural policy ([Regulation (EU) 2021/2116](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2021/2116/oj)) ([Implementing Regulation (EU) 2022/128](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg_impl/2022/128/oj)).

Eco-schemes, agri-environment-climate commitments (AECCs) and investments are governed by:

*   rules on support for Strategic Plans drawn up by EU countries under the common agricultural policy ([Regulation (EU) 2021/2115](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2021/2115/oj)).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Biodiversity refers to the variety of life forms, including genetic, species, and ecosystem diversity, that are essential for maintaining ecological balance and agricultural sustainability.
Biodiversity in agriculture enhances ecosystem services, increases resilience against pests and diseases, and ensures food security by providing diverse genetic resources for crops and livestock.
This report explores the role of biodiversity in agro-ecosystems, its benefits for agriculture, and the challenges posed by modern agricultural practices on ecological sustainability.
